---
title: "Unit 2a — Probability & Estimation Foundations (I)"
subtitle: "Poisson probabilities · Bernoulli MLE · Fisher information"
engine: markdown
---

## Overview

This unit lays three foundations of estimation and ties them together. You'll work with a
**Poisson distribution** (the probability of a count), the **maximum-likelihood estimator** of
a Bernoulli success rate, and the **Fisher information** that sets how precisely that rate can
be estimated. The through-line: the MLE's variance *is* the Cramér–Rao bound $1/(N\,I(r))$, so
Part C tells you exactly how good the Part B estimator can be.

## Learning objectives

By the end of Unit 2a you will be able to:

- **Compute Poisson probabilities** $P(X=x)=\lambda^x e^{-\lambda}/x!$ and a range probability
  with its complement. *(Apply)*
- **Derive the Bernoulli MLE** $\hat r = \frac1N\sum_n x_n$ — likelihood, unique maximum,
  solution. *(Apply/Analyze)*
- **Derive the Fisher information** $I(r) = 1/(r(1-r))$. *(Analyze)*
- **Interpret** each result: how the estimator behaves, how informative one observation is,
  and the limits. *(Evaluate)*

## What you'll do

Three parts:

- **(A) Poisson probabilities** *(code)* — implement the Poisson pmf (standard-library `math`
  only) and use it to compute $P(2\le X\le 6)$ for $\lambda=3$ and its complement.
- **(B) Bernoulli MLE** *(derivation)* — for an i.i.d. binary sample, show the likelihood,
  that the stationary point is a unique maximum (strict concavity), and the closed-form
  solution.
- **(C) Fisher information** *(derivation)* — for a single Bernoulli observation: definition,
  score, curvature, expectation.

Then answer three short interpretation questions. **Any valid derivation route earns full
credit** (e.g. the score-variance form of $I(r)$); state your assumptions.

## Prerequisites & connections

**Review first.** Basic probability (pmf, expectation, independence), logarithms, and
single-variable calculus (differentiate, find a maximum). For Part A, Unit 0's toolchain.

**Connections.**

> The Bernoulli/Beta machinery here returns throughout the course: the MLE's variance attains
> the **Cramér–Rao bound** (Part C), the same estimator is proven **unbiased in Unit 2b**, and
> the Bernoulli rate becomes a Bayesian quantity with a Beta prior in **Unit 4** and a logistic
> model in **Unit 5**.

## How it's graded

This unit is graded by **specifications, not points**, against three bundles. Here
**Correctness** (the core/gateway bundle) holds two kinds of check: Part A is **autograded** by
`test_hw.py`, while the Part B and C **derivations are graded step-by-step by a human** (each
step passes at *Proficient* or better). **Interpretation** scores your CERL answers (Claim,
Evidence, Reasoning, Limits) and **Process** covers reproducibility and the submission
contract. Any unmet bundle gets one **revise-and-resubmit**; autograded Correctness
resubmissions are unlimited until the deadline. Full policy in [`GRADING.md`](../GRADING.md).

## What to submit

- Your completed **`hw.py`** — `poisson`, `calculate_poisson_pmf_a`,
  `calculate_poisson_pmf_b` (signatures unchanged, `math` only).
- Your **derivation write-ups** — the Bernoulli MLE (B1–B3) and the Fisher information
  (C1–C4), rendered in the unit.
- Your **interpretation answers** (I1–I3).

*Submit via the course page — [submit mechanism TBD].*

## Deadline & revision window

- Due: **[PLACEHOLDER]**
- Post-feedback revision window: **[PLACEHOLDER]**

## Estimated effort

Roughly **4–6 hours [PROPOSED]** — Part A is short; most of the time goes into the two
derivations and writing clear interpretations.
